front: Poem: in the 'Instead of a Preface' section of this poem, a woman with 'lips blue from the cold' asks the speaker 'Can you describe this?' while they wait in a prison line
Poem: the 'Crucifixion' section of this poem begins 'Weep not for me, mother' and contrasts Mary Magdalene tearing her hair with the 'silent and alone' Virgin Mary
Poem: 'The Sentence' section of this poem states 'I have so much to do today: I must kill memory once and for all'
Poem: this poem speaks of 'a hundred million people' shouting through the speaker's 'tortured mouth'
Poem: this poem ends with a prison dove cooing as 'ships sail softly down' the Neva
Poem: this poem was shared via samizdat; its author would write lines, have friends memorize them, then burn the slips of paper
Poem: this poem's Epilogue states 'I'd like to call you all by name, but the list has been removed'
Poem: this poem opens 'I have lit my treasured candles, one by one' and is partly set at the Fontanka House during the Leningrad siege
Poem: this poem asks 'What have poets, in any case, to do with sin?' and features a masquerade ball attended by Faust, Dorian Gray, and Thomas Glahn
Poem: in this poem, the 'Guest from the Future' represents Isaiah Berlin, who visited the poet at the Fontanka House in 1945
Poem: this Akhmatova poem asks 'who will grieve for this woman?' and describes 'a single glance: a sudden dart of pain / stitching her eyes before she made a sound'
Author: this poet was a leading figure of the Acmeist movement, which met at the Stray Dog Cafe and published in the journal Apollon
Author: Andrei Zhdanov called this Russian poet 'half-nun, half-harlot'
Author: this poet's first husband was Nikolay Gumilev, co-founder of Acmeism, who was executed by the Cheka in 1921
Author: Isaiah Berlin visited this poet in a mythologized 1945 all-night meeting, after which she collapsed and said 'I am in love'
Author: this poet lived at the Fontanka House (Sheremet'ev Palace), now the site of her literary museum
Author: Marina Tsvetaeva called this poet a 'muse of lament' and wrote 'I give you my town of many bells, and with the gift: my heart'
Author: Osip Mandelstam compared this poet to Cassandra in a poem beginning 'I was not then questing, / Cassandra, for your lips'
